title: Cloud Connection: Working Together Digitally!
subtitle: share, sync, create!
overview: Students will learn the power of real-time cloud collaboration by working simultaneously on shared documents, managing access permissions, and using digital communication tools to complete a team project without borders.

images._note: Add screenshots from the cloud tools lab session — multi-cursor views, version histories, and permission menus.

gallery.caption: Teamwork in Sync — Divided tasks merging instantly on screen

keyTopics: The Cloud Workspace — The Public Sandbox | Files living securely on the internet rather than trapped on one hard drive
Real-Time Syncing — The Mirror Effect | Every letter you type updates immediately on your teammate's screen
Access Control — Security Shields | Deciding who can modify your data (Editor) versus who can only read it (Viewer)
Version History — The Time Machine | Reviewing past edits to see who wrote what and fixing accidental mistakes
Comments & Tagging — Digital Sticky Notes | Leaving feedback and using @mentions to assign tasks directly to partners
Digital Etiquette — Netiquette | Being respectful in shared spaces and not deleting a teammate's hard work

activity.title: The Cloud Kingdom: Co-Authoring a Digital Encyclopedia
parts: Computers or Tablets with active internet connections
Google Workspace or Microsoft 365 school accounts
Project Assignment Matrix (assigned roles: Writer, Designer, Editor)
Shared master link provided by the classroom instructor
steps: Log into your workstation and open the shared document link sent by the teacher.
Identify your teammate's custom colored cursor on screen and wave to each other via text!
Claim your assigned page section—do not type over or replace anyone else's designated workspace.
Work together to write a 3-paragraph summary on an interesting animal, with writers inputting facts and designers dropping images.
Highlight a teammate's sentence and insert a constructive feedback comment using the '+' or '@' feature.
Open up the Version History sidebar to trace how your file evolved from a blank page into a finished project.
Challenge: Change your document setting link from 'Editor' to 'Viewer' and test if a partner can still type on your page.

quiz.q: Where are your files physically saved when you use collaborative tools like Google Docs or Word Online?
options: On your computer's local desktop hard drive
On a physical USB flash drive plugged into your monitor
On remote cloud servers accessible securely through the internet
Inside the monitor's built-in memory bank
quiz.explanation: Cloud tools save files on secure, massive remote servers instead of your local machine. This allows any device with an internet connection to log in and access them!

quiz.q: What happens if two students type on the exact same line of a cloud document at the exact same moment?
options: The computer crashes and deletes all user accounts
Both edits display live on screen simultaneously in real-time
The file locks up and prevents editing for 24 hours
The internet connection drops automatically for safety
quiz.explanation: Real-time synchronization allows edits to merge instantly! You will see your partner's unique colored cursor jumping around as they type right alongside you.

quiz.q: If you want to share a project template with a classmate so they can read it but NOT alter or mess up your words, which permission level should you select?
options: Editor
Owner
Viewer
Administrator
quiz.explanation: The 'Viewer' setting allows individuals to read the information but locks down the page so they cannot delete text, type additions, or change layout graphics.

quiz.q: Oh no! A teammate accidentally deleted a giant paragraph of research right before submission. How can you fix it?
options: Panic and rewrite the entire document from absolute scratch
Open Version History to locate and restore the previous saved version from an hour ago
Throw away the computer and request a new account configuration
The text is gone forever because saving is automatic in the cloud
quiz.explanation: Cloud suites track every single keystroke automatically. Version history allows you to look back in time and completely restore previous iterations of your document if something goes wrong.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an example of GOOD digital etiquette (Netiquette) when collaborating inside a group workspace?
options: Deleting your friend's section as a funny practical joke
Changing the main heading font size to 100 to annoy your team
Using the Comments feature to communicate adjustments before rewriting a partner's work
Writing random words on random slides to fill up space quickly
quiz.explanation: Respecting other users' boundaries is vital in online collaboration. Communication tools like comments let you coordinate revisions harmoniously without overstepping your bounds.
